title: From The Temporary To The Eternal Lessons From The Life Of The Apostle Paul
scriptureForWeek: This week is an overview of various passages where Paul urges his disciples to use their time in light of eternity.
weekTheme: Boys squander; Men redeem God will give us opportunities The typical person lives for around 4000 weeks. A normal human life consists of 500 million breaths for three billion heartbeats. That’s it. All the things you do in your life will take space in those short weeks with those breaths and beats. And that is an assumption that you are going to get all of those weeks. You may die in a car accident. You may get cancer. You may get injured in a workplace incident. Your whole life as a man will come down to what you do with that time. And it gets even heavier than that. What you do with your time will have eternal ramifications. Those 4000 weeks will shape what you do for the next 4000 years, or 4000 million years, or eternity. So what you do with your time and how you learn to value it is a central issue for you as you grow into a man. Money is not the most important asset a man possesses, nor is power, fame, popularity, or love. Time is a man’s most important asset. Henry David Thoreau said, “The cost of a thing is the amount of what I will call life which is required to be exchanged for it.” That’s ultimately what you are doing as a man, making an exchange of your life for the things you choose to do. The clock of life is only wound once. You have to learn to make good exchanges from what doesn’t matter to what really does. Yet so many guys your age never really think about time from a mature perspective, let alone an eternal one. We kind of live like we have all

days.title: God And Time
days.scripture: GALATIANS 4:4-5 But when the right time came, God sent his Son, born of a woman, subject to the law. God sent him to buy freedom for us who were slaves to the law, so that he could adopt us as his very own children.
days.reflection: What time is it? Have you ever had to ask someone that because you didn’t have a watch? Knowing what time it is is important because it determines the context of how you act. You act differently during school time and chill time. Different during the day and night. Different in an exam and hanging with your friends. But on a larger level, way larger, what time is it? Who is determining what happens in our world today? Who determines what leaders come into power? Who determines what technologies emerge? Who determines which nations rise and fall? Is life just a random series of events? Is anyone in control of what happens in our lives and what happens in our world? Do leaders, governments, countries, and nations just pop up and down based on human power and dynamics? If you were to ask people who don’t know God, their answer would be yes. We are just accidents here, and history is driven by the accidental rise and fall of nations. There is no divine plan, time, or seasons at all. But if you were to ask Paul who was in charge of time, he would confidently reply that God was. Today's verse talks about why Jesus came when he did. Why he was born 2000 years ago and not today. Paul calls it “the right time.” Another translation calls it “the fullness of time.” It reminds us that God is working behind the scenes and that we are secure in his story. Acts 17:26-28 says, “From one man he made all the nations, that they should inhabit the whole earth; and he marked out their appointed times in history and the boundaries of their lands. God did this so that they would seek him and perhaps reach out for him and find him, though he is not far from any one of us. ‘For in him we live and move and have our being." You are here for a reason. God determined the exact place you would live and the exact place you would be born into. God is arranging human history and it's not out of control, run by powerful men, or controlled by the devil. God is over time. And he cares about your specific time. Psalms 31:15 says, “My times are in your hands.” You don’t have to freak out about your life. Your birth, life, and even your death are under God's control. You can trust him. Jesus said something even more comforting in John 10:28-29, “I give them eternal life, and they shall never perish; no one will snatch them out of my hand. My Father, who has given them to me, is greater than all; no one can snatch them out of my Father’s hand.” Your time is in God’s hands, and no one can snatch you out of God’s hands. You are secure in him. You can take great comfort as you head into manhood that you are heading into a plan and purpose God has made for you. You can trust him that you won't miss out, and you are here for a reason. God is over all, and that’s good news.
questions: What is your favorite time of year? Why?
What comfort can you get from the fact that God is sovereign over all things and in control of history? What comfort can you get from the fact that your time is in his hands?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share how they first learned to have an eternal view of time, and not just a secular one. How did it shift their thinking?

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. Look here, you who say, “Today or tomorrow we are going to a certain town and will stay there a year. We will do business there and make a profit.” How do you know what your life will be like tomorrow? Your life is like the morning fog—it’s here a little while, then it’s gone. What you ought to say is, “If the Lord wants us to, we will live and do this or that.” Otherwise you are boasting about your own pretentious plans, and all such boasting is evil. James 4:13-16 Read through these verses slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to tell God that you trust him. Ask him to keep revealing what he has in store for you in this season.

days.title: The Gift Of Time
days.scripture: ACTS 17:22-28 So Paul, standing before the council, addressed them as follows: “Men of Athens, I notice that you are very religious in every way, for as I was walking along I saw your many shrines. And one of your altars had this inscription on it: ‘To an Unknown God.’ This God, whom you worship without knowing, is the one I’m telling you about. “He is the God who made the world and everything in it. Since he is Lord of heaven and earth, he doesn’t live in man-made temples, and human hands can’t serve his needs—for he has no needs. He himself gives life and breath to everything, and he satisfies every need. From one man he created all the nations throughout the whole earth. He decided beforehand when they should rise and fall, and he determined their boundaries. “His purpose was for the nations to seek after God and perhaps feel their way toward him and find him—though he is not far from any one of us. For in him we live and move and exist. As some of your own poets have said, ‘We are his offspring.’
days.reflection: If you were given the chance to speak in front of all the government officials in the nation's capital, with all the media present and watching, what would you say to them? What would your message be? Would you talk to them about the economy, gun violence, the environment, or the dangers of social media? Would you talk to them about religion, drug problems or anxiety amongst teenagers today? It would be a heavy responsibility to try to figure out what to put in that speech. In today’s reading, the Apostle Paul was actually given that chance. While he was in the city of Athens, the intellectual capital of the world, he was invited by the leaders of the city to give a speech about what he believed. What Paul shared was controversial and challenging. He didn’t shrink back, and he didn’t talk about trivial things. Paul wanted the leaders in Athens to get one thing clearly in their minds. Their time on earth was a gift from God, and they needed to see him as the source of it. He wanted them to know that their time on earth was not theirs to do whatever they wanted with, but a sacred trust given by God to fulfill His purpose. He said these interesting lines. He himself gives life and breath to everything… God is the one who has put you here. He has given you your very existence. Your time on this earth is a divine gift and not a random accident. He decided beforehand when they should rise and fall, and he determined their boundaries. God had put you in the exact moment of history that you are meant to be in, in the country he wants you to live. Your time on earth is connected to God’s larger timing for all of human history. You are in a story way, way, way bigger than you think. Zoom out and reflect on that. For in him we live and move and exist. Your life is happening inside the story of God. You only exist because he exists. Without God, your family, your friends, the whole world wouldn’t even be here. This should produce gratitude and humility in your heart. Guys your age need to hear Paul’s message again. Our nation needs to hear this message again too. Your time is given to you by God. You didn’t come up with it, and you don’t get to determine what’s best to do with it. Becoming a man is about becoming grateful. It's about seeking out what God wants you to do with the gift that is your life. It's about taking seriously the fact that you will give an account for your life and seeking to know him is the most important part of it. In our secular world, people always talk about rights. They talk about human rights, women’s rights, LGBTQ rights, the rights of the poor, and religious rights. But life is not a right, it’s a gift. Forgetting that makes a man selfish, entitled, and ungrateful. But remembering it makes you humble, full of gratitude, and attuned to God's purpose. Learning to see your time here is a gift is what real men do. That’s the kind of message you should share with others if you are ever given the chance.
questions: Do you tend to view your life as a gift or a right? Why?
Why do you think it’s important that you view your time on earth as a gift? What can go wrong in a man’s life if he doesn’t view his time properly?
Where do you see things going wrong in our world today because people refuse to see their time as something they are accountable to God for?

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. Yes, they knew God, but they wouldn’t worship him as God or even give him thanks. And they began to think up foolish ideas of what God was like. As a result, their minds became dark and confused. Claiming to be wise, they instead became utter fools.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God for the gift of your life. Spend a moment acknowledging him as your loving creator and thank him for what he has given you.

days.title: Focused For The Fight
days.scripture: 2 TIMOTHY 2:3-7 Endure suffering along with me, as a good soldier of Christ Jesus. Soldiers don’t get tied up in the affairs of civilian life, for then they cannot please the officer who enlisted them. And athletes cannot win the prize unless they follow the rules. And hardworking farmers should be the first to enjoy the fruit of their labor. Think about what I am saying. The Lord will help you understand all these things.
days.reflection: I hate to tell you this, but you live in the most distracted generation in history. The world today makes it almost impossible to focus on anything for long. As a result, guys your age can struggle to pay attention and miss out on the most important things happening around them. You can get sucked into the trivial things that don’t really matter and end up wasting your life. It happens little by little without you even knowing it. In today’s reading, Paul wrote to Timothy, one of his closest friends and leaders of the church in Ephesus to find men who could help him lead as he was going through a hard time. Paul gave Timothy a series of qualifications and characteristics to look out for in these men. He lists one of the most important characteristics of the men we need today too: men who can focus on the most important things without getting sucked into the things that don’t really matter. Paul uses the metaphor of a soldier to make his point. Soldiers need to be focused. Soldiers need to be able to discern what matters in the moment. Soldiers need to be able to discern between what is important and irrelevant, the urgent and the trivial, the temporary and the eternal. There is nothing worse than a distracted soldier. He puts his own life in danger and the lives of the men around him. It’s even worse when a soldier gets caught up in civilian affairs. When he gets sucked into things that aren’t appropriate for a man in the middle of a war. When he loses sight of what matters and starts focusing on the wrong things. If you are going to be a man who uses your time well, you are going to have to avoid trivial things. You are going to have to learn to live on mission and not get sucked into civilian affairs. Civilian affairs aren’t bad things in themselves, they are just not appropriate in a war. There are so many distractions that want to suck you in and cause you to lose focus. Social media can be one of them, porn is a dangerous one, excessive video games can take up all your time, and so can watching too many shows on Netflix. These can make you miss out on the most important lessons you need to get at this point in your life. They can delay your development, hinder your maturity, and make you miss the moment. At this stage of your life, it's important you focus on: What God's word says, not what the secular media says. Growing in discipline, not giving in to your feelings or bodily desires. Learning about God’s grace, and not self-righteous legalism. How to play the long game, and not compromise in the moment. How to think big picture, and not with a small-minded perspective. God wants to train you to live a life that matters. A life of consequence. A life of importance. It starts by focusing on his calling and not civilian affairs. You are doing so well. You have almost finished a whole year of The Primal Path and you have made massive leaps and strides. Don’t shrink back; keep going. It will pay off way more than you can ever realize now. You will hear the “well done” you ache for from your commanding officer. Hold your head high young man, and keep walking forward.
questions: A.W. Tozer, a pastor from a previous generation said this: “When you kill time, remember that it has no resurrection.”
What do you think he means by this, and why do you think it's important to learn that lesson at this stage of your life?
What are some things that tend to distract you? What are some things you can do to push back on those distractions?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share how they learned to focus on what matters and resist trivial things. What difference has it made, and what advice do they have for you?

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. So don’t worry about these things, saying, "What will we eat? What will we drink? What will we wear?" These things dominate the thoughts of unbelievers, but your heavenly Father already knows all your needs. Seek the Kingdom of God above all else, and live righteously, and he will give you everything you need. “So don’t worry about tomorrow, for tomorrow will bring its own worries. Today’s trouble is enough for today. Matthew 6:31-34 Read through these verses slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to set you free from any distractions you may be caught in. Ask him for fresh vision and focus.

days.title: The Power Of Regret
days.scripture: 1 TIMOTHY 1:12-17 I thank Christ Jesus our Lord, who has given me strength to do his work. He considered me trustworthy and appointed me to serve him, even though I used to blaspheme the name of Christ. In my insolence, I persecuted his people. But God had mercy on me because I did it in ignorance and unbelief. Oh, how generous and gracious our Lord was! He filled me with the faith and love that come from Christ Jesus. This is a trustworthy saying, and everyone should accept it: “Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners”—and I am the worst of them all. But God had mercy on me so that Christ Jesus could use me as a prime example of his great patience with even the worst sinners. Then others will realize that they, too, can believe in him and receive eternal life. All honor and glory to God forever and ever! He is the eternal King, the unseen one who never dies; he alone is God. Amen.
days.reflection: Have you ever made a decision that you regretted? Have you ever done something you wished with all your heart that you could go back in time and undo? Regret is one of the most powerful forces in a man’s life. It can haunt him, teach him, and inspire him to change. To live well as a man is to learn to avoid regret and to learn from regret. The Apostle Paul had regrets in his life. He regretted persecuting the church. He regretted the stoning of Stephen. He regretted hating Jesus. But Paul used those regrets in his life to live with passion and redeem the time. He used them as fuel to move forward. Several years back, a group of researchers conducted the world’s largest study on regret. They asked thousands of people from around the world what things they most regret from their lives. They took all the answers and broke them down into the highest-ranked categories. Psychology Today reported these regrets as follows. 1-Foundation Regrets. These regrets represent failures to be responsible, hardworking, or diligent growing up. They leave you thinking, “If only I had done the work and not cheated or done things half-heartedly.” You are in a season where you are laying the foundation for your life. The foundation of your health, your friendships, your education, your finances. Here is the lesson: Think ahead. Do the work. Start now. 2-Boldness Regrets. Over time, men begin to take less risks. They lose their edge and their boldness. They get comfortable and practical. They get afraid of taking chances. They don’t step out and step up. This kind of regret leaves you thinking, “If only I had taken that chance.” The lesson: Take the risk. Talk to the girl. Share your faith. Go on the trip. 3-Moral Regrets. This category represents those times when you made bad choices that went against your values and convictions. This may have been out of fear of missing out, fear of what others think, or apathy. These kinds of regrets are the most painful. Think back to the lives of the men you have studied so far. Joseph's brothers' regrets for selling him into slavery, Moses's regrets for killing the Egyptian, David’s regrets for committing adultery with Bathsheba, and Paul’s regrets for persecuting the Christians. These regrets leave you thinking, “If only I had done the right thing.” The lesson: When in doubt, do the right thing. Don’t care what others think. Hold to your convictions. 4-Connection Regrets. These regrets happen when you neglect the people who matter to you and live a selfish life. These regrets made up the largest category of all regrets. That’s because life is really about relationships. At your age, you can take relationships for granted with your friends, family, and even God. But these will break your heart over time. These regrets sound like, “If only I had reached out.” The lesson: Learn to love people well. Don’t be selfish. Invest in others. God can forgive our mistakes and bring beauty out of brokenness, but it's even better to avoid doing things we regret in the first place. As you grow and move forward in your life, remember that time moves in one direction and you can’t go back and change things. Be wise. Think carefully. Avoid regret. Time can be a blessing and a curse depending on how you use it. Ask God for wisdom to avoid regrets. Lay the right foundation now. Take the risks. Follow your convictions and live well. That will be a life well lived.
questions: What is one of the biggest regrets you have in your life so far?
If you could go back in time and change something from this past week, what would it be and why?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a regret they have in each of the 4 categories of regret, and what they have learned from them.

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. Do not remember the rebellious sins of my youth. Remember me in the light of your unfailing love, for you are merciful, O LORD. The LORD is good and does what is right; he shows the proper path to those who go astray. He leads the humble in doing right teaching them his way. Psalms 25:7-9 Read through these verses slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God for his mercy in the areas you have regrets. Ask him for wisdom to live well, make good decisions, and avoid regret.

days.title: Seizing The Moment
days.scripture: COLOSSIANS 4:5-6 Live wisely among those who are not believers, and make the most of every opportunity. Let your conversation be gracious and attractive so that you will have the right response for everyone.
days.reflection: Have you heard the Latin phrase “Carpe Diem” before? It is roughly translated as “seize the day.” It’s a reminder to make the most of the fleeting moments of our lives that are flying by us every second. You will never get this moment again. You will never get this day again. Each moment in our lives is a one-off. It’s filled with the eternal possibility of eternal regret. In today’s reading, the Apostle Paul urged the believers in the church of Colossae to live wisely in their culture that didn’t believe in God and to make the most of the opportunities they had to share his life. Paul was urging them to have a godly vision of carpe diem. Learning to seize the day is an important part of growing into a man. Boys tend to think they have all the time in the world. But men realize there are certain moments and seasons that require us to act. There are windows of time and key moments that only come once. Living well is about knowing when those are and taking action while you can. Paul wanted the Colossians to be wise and to be aware of the moments to share God's love with the people around them. When it comes to walking with God, timing is so important. And when it comes to reaching out to share the good news about Jesus with others, we need to do this while others’ hearts are open. That’s why Paul wrote in 2 Corinthians 6:1-2, As God’s partners, we beg you not to accept this marvelous gift of God’s kindness and then ignore it. For God says, “At just the right time, I heard you. On the day of salvation, I helped you. “Indeed, the “right time” is now. Today is the day of salvation.” The author of Hebrews wrote, “Today if you hear his voice, do not harden your heart.” As you grow into a man, you are going to learn the importance and power of discerning and seizing moments. You are going to have to learn to step out in boldness when the moment calls for it. You are going to need to listen to the voice of God for when and where to act. One of the most amazing things about Jesus is how well he understood the power of moments. He knew when it was the right time to withdraw and seek God and the right time to preach and pour himself out. He knew when it was the right time to turn the tables over and the right time to turn the other cheek. He knew when it was the right time to raise his voice and the right time to remain silent. He knew when it was time to speak up and when to shut up. So much of Jesus’ life was about getting the moment right. Jesus wants to disciple you on how to use your time well too. This week you have looked at what Paul taught about an eternal view of time. You have seen how God is sovereign over time. You have seen how time is a gift. You have seen how to avoid distractions and use your time well. You have looked at how to avoid regrets by making wise decisions in the moment, and how to see and seize the right opportunities at the right time. Now the question is if you will put this into practice. You only have a limited amount of time on this earth to prepare for eternity. These days matter. These moments have the power to shape you forever. Learn from Paul, learn from Jesus, and learn to use your time well. Your eternity depends on it. Carpe diem young man.
questions: Do you tend to seize moments or let them pass you by? Why or why not?
Why do you think it’s so important to learn to think about time from an eternal perspective at your age? What can happen if you get this right now? What can happen if you get this wrong?

Ask the person you are doing this study with to share about a time when they missed a moment because they failed to seize it, and about a time they stepped up and grabbed the moment in front of them. What have they learned about Carpe Diem as they have grown older?

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. See that no one is sexually immoral, or is godless like Esau, who for a single meal sold his inheritance rights as the oldest son. Afterward, as you know, when he wanted to inherit this blessing, he was rejected. Even though he sought the blessing with tears, he could not change what he had done. Hebrews 12:16-17 Read through these verses slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you boldness to step up and seize the opportunities and moments he puts in front of you.

extras: WEEK 6 MANSCHOOL BUCKET LIST MAN SCHOOL REMINDER The recipe for fun is pretty simple when you’re raising boys: add to any activity an element of danger, stir in a little exploration, add a dash of destruction, and you’ve got yourself a winner. - John Eldredge OPTION 1. ACTIVITY 1: BUCKET LIST Our lives will be over before we know it. So will the time your son has in your home. Spend an evening brainstorming a bucket list of all the things you guys want to do before he leaves your home. ACTIVITY 2: 4000 WEEKS You can also take some time and fill these out together. Have him figure out and fill in how much time he has, and then you fill out how much time you have. A striking and practical tool to help you think about the big picture of your lives. https://tinyurl.com/2s3m7k84 https://tinyurl.com/yc22k3s9 OPTION 2. WATCH WE BOUGHT A ZOO A great movie about taking risks, avoiding regret, and making bold moves. Heartwarming and a great scene about seizing moments with 20 seconds of courage. https://youtu.be/Krh1koDU2uE OPTION 3. DINNER AND DISCUSS Take your son out to one of his favorite restaurants or for desserts and talk through what it means to use his time well. This is a great time to talk about the power of regret, eternity, and seizing moments. Often young guys think they have all the time in the world, but their teenage years and journey on The Primal Path will be over before they know it.
FIELD NOTES PRAYER FOR MY SON
